serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of paprika powder is approximately 1 teaspoon (about 2.3 g), used as a seasoning or garnish in cooking.

nutrient_source.title: Paprika, spices — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 02028)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry for paprika (spice) used as primary nutrient anchor. Input HPB values cross-referenced; energy (282 kcal), protein (14.14 g), fat (12.89 g), carbohydrate (53.99 g), dietary fibre (34.9 g), sodium (68 mg), potassium (2280 mg), calcium (229 mg), iron (21.14 mg), sugar (10.34 g) are consistent with USDA SR Legacy data for paprika and are retained. Saturated fat sourced from USDA SR Legacy (1.98 g/100g). Cholesterol confirmed 0 mg per USDA SR Legacy.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of spices and condiments — analogous estimate based on low-carbohydrate-availability dried spice category; reference: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for paprika powder exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 15 (low) based on: (1) paprika is a dried ground spice with very high dietary fibre (34.9 g/100g) that substantially reduces glycaemic availability; (2) typical serving size is 1–5 g, yielding negligible glycaemic load; (3) analogous dried spice/herb category entries in the International Tables of Glycemic Index (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283) consistently fall in the low GI range (<20). GL per serving calculated as: GI × (available carbohydrate per serving in g) / 100 = 15 × (0.44) / 100 ≈ 0.07, rounded to 0.1.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (68 mg/100g) and exceptionally high potassium (2280 mg/100g) with a Na:K ratio strongly favourable for blood pressure management
hypertension.guidance: Paprika powder is highly suitable for hypertensive individuals; used in typical spice quantities (1–5 g per dish), it contributes negligible sodium (<3.4 mg per serving) while its high potassium content supports DASH dietary principles, and it can serve as a flavour-enhancing salt substitute.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Negligible glycaemic load per serving due to gram-level use, high dietary fibre (34.9 g/100g), and bioactive capsaicin with potential insulin-sensitising effects
type2_diabetes.guidance: Despite a moderate carbohydrate content per 100 g, paprika powder is used in 1–5 g quantities per dish, yielding a glycaemic load of less than 0.1 per serving; its high fibre and capsaicin content may additionally support glycaemic control, making it a safe and beneficial seasoning for people with Type 2 Diabetes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Rich in antioxidants (vitamin E 29.1 mg/100g, vitamin A 520 mcg RAE/100g, capsanthin, beta-carotene), low saturated fat (1.98 g/100g), and high potassium supporting vascular health
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Paprika powder is cardioprotective when used as a culinary spice; its high antioxidant load (carotenoids, vitamin E), low saturated fat, negligible cholesterol, and high potassium content collectively support endothelial health and reduce oxidative LDL modification, with no meaningful contribution to dietary sodium or saturated fat at typical serving sizes.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High antioxidant and anti-inflammatory phytonutrient content (capsaicin, carotenoids, vitamin C) offset by nightshade classification requiring individual assessment
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Paprika is rich in anti-inflammatory compounds including capsaicin, beta-carotene, and vitamin C; however, as a nightshade (Capsicum annuum), RA patients with known solanine sensitivity should trial elimination and monitor symptom response before regular use.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Paprika powder is a finely ground dried spice produced from red Capsicum annuum peppers, characterised by its vivid red colour, fine dry texture, and flavour profile ranging from mild and sweet to smoky depending on variety (sweet paprika, smoked paprika / pimentón). It is a staple seasoning in Hungarian, Spanish, Turkish, and Moroccan cuisines, and is widely used globally as a colouring and flavouring agent in spice rubs, stews, soups, rice dishes, marinades, and garnishes. Nutritionally, paprika powder is exceptionally dense per 100 g — providing 282 kcal, 14.14 g protein, 12.89 g fat, 53.99 g carbohydrate, and a remarkable 34.9 g dietary fibre — but these values are not clinically meaningful at typical culinary use levels of 1–5 g per dish. It is an outstanding source of vitamin E (29.1 mg/100g), vitamin A (520 mcg RAE/100g), potassium (2280 mg/100g), iron (21.14 mg/100g), and calcium (229 mg/100g). Its very low sodium (68 mg/100g) and high potassium make it strongly DASH-aligned. Capsaicin and carotenoid pigments (capsanthin, beta-carotene) confer antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties relevant to cardiovascular and metabolic health. Glycaemic load per serving is negligible (<0.1). Paprika is naturally gluten-free, vegan, halal, and kosher. As a nightshade, it should be used cautiously by individuals with solanine sensitivity, particularly those with rheumatoid arthritis.
common_user_queries: Is paprika powder good for diabetes?
Is paprika powder high in sodium?
What is the GI of paprika?
Can I use paprika if I have high blood pressure?
How many calories in paprika powder?

colouring agent in sauces and marinades
ingredient in spice blends (e.g., ras el hanout, berbere)

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced from HPB Singapore and cross-validated against USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 102028 for paprika (spice); values are consistent across both approved sources. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and micronutrient values sourced directly from USDA SR Legacy. GI is an analogous estimate documented with peer-reviewed basis. Ontology codes are defensible and cross-checked.
notes: Cholesterol is confirmed zero (0.0 mg) per USDA SR Legacy FDC 102028 and HPB data — paprika is a plant-derived spice containing no cholesterol; this is naturally zero. Trans fat is 0.0 g — naturally zero for a minimally processed plant spice. Vitamin B12 is 0.0 mcg and vitamin D is 0.0 mcg — naturally zero for a plant-derived food. monounsaturated_fat_g (1.69 g), polyunsaturated_fat_g (7.77 g), trans_fat_g (0.0 g) sourced from USDA SR Legacy FDC 102028. GI is an analogous estimate (GI ~15, low) based on dried spice category data from Atkinson et al. (2008) International Tables of Glycemic Index, Diabetes Care; no direct GI measurement for paprika exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. Typical culinary serving is 1–5 g; per-100g nutrient density values are not representative of actual dietary intake. Paprika is a nightshade (Capsicum annuum); individuals with nightshade sensitivity, particularly those with rheumatoid arthritis, should use cautiously. Smoked or flavoured paprika blends may contain added salt or sugar; label review is recommended for sodium-restricted diets.
